chunk_summary: Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) is recommended as first-line induction therapy for pediatric Crohn’s disease, promoting remission, growth, and avoiding steroid toxicity. Monitoring and staged calorie advancement are crucial to prevent complications like refeeding syndrome.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) in inducing remission in pediatric Crohn’s disease.
Learn how to prescribe and monitor EEN, including formula type, duration, and exclusivity.
Recognize the importance of preventing refeeding syndrome in malnourished children during EEN therapy.
Explore emerging food-based approaches for Crohn’s disease management and their limitations.
key_facts: Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) is recommended for 6–8 weeks as first-line induction therapy for active luminal Crohn’s disease.
Polymeric formulas are preferred due to better palatability and cost compared to elemental formulas.
EEN should be prescribed with documented exclusivity, formula type, daily volume, and monitoring of growth and inflammatory markers.
Refeeding syndrome prevention requires staged calorie advancement and electrolyte monitoring in children with severe wasting.
Emerging food-based approaches, such as the Crohn’s Disease Exclusion Diet combined with partial enteral nutrition, show promise but require further validation.
reference_quote: Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) is recommended as first-line induction therapy for active luminal Crohn’s disease in pediatric patients. (ECCO-ESPGHAN guideline update for paediatric Crohn’s disease, 2020)
document_key_topics: Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) for pediatric Crohn’s disease
Monitoring and preventing complications like refeeding syndrome
Emerging food-based approaches for Crohn’s disease management
